misconceptionSummary: Students double a number when asked to square it, or triple it when asked to cube it.

visuals.ruleText: n² = n × n   |   n³ = n × n × n
visuals.subText: The exponent tells you how many times to use n as a factor — not a multiplier

steps.narration: Squaring means multiplying a number by itself. Cubing means multiplying a number by itself three times. The exponent tells you how many copies of the number to multiply together — not how many times to add it.

Five squared is five times five, which is twenty-five — not ten. Three cubed is three times three times three: nine times three is twenty-seven — not nine. The exponent counts multiplications, not the multiplier.

The square root of twenty-five is five because five squared is twenty-five. The cube root of twenty-seven is three because three cubed is twenty-seven. Roots undo powers — they are inverse operations.

visuals.label: Square = multiply by itself once.  Cube = multiply by itself twice more.

steps.narration: Twenty-five, twenty-seven, five, three. Squaring and cubing are repeated multiplication — roots are the inverse, asking which number was multiplied by itself.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: 5² = 10 (doubled instead of squared). Squaring means 5 × 5 = 25. The exponent 2 means use 5 as a factor twice — it does not mean multiply by 2.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: The exponent tells you how many times to use the number as a factor — not how many times to add or multiply it by. Five squared is five times five, not five times two.
